CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Our readers remember Riverstown and the defiance
and eventual ‘defeat of Horatius at the hands ofthe
grand army of Irish Ireland. Egerton, the imported
Secretary at the G.P.O., Dublin, has now—happily for
Irish Ireland-—undismayed by the fate of Horatius of
Riverstown, stepped into the breach at the G.P.O.,
Dublin. - He has struck the pole of combat outside the
door of Irish Treland. Poor Mr. Egerton, who being a
Britisher, does not know this country, has done the

* right thing—from the Irish Ireland point of view! The
war with the anti-Irish post office needed nothing so
much as this timely interference of the G.P.0., Dublin,
Hundreds of parcels addressed in.Irish from the head'
quarters of the Gaelic League were turned away from
the doors; thousands of parcels and letters will, of
course, be the reply back straight between the eyes of
Mr. Egerton, the imported Secretary of the G.P.O.,

ublin. Perhaps Mr. Egerton is only a factotum, and

may be only carrying out the instructions of his British
masters; but we mention his name as he is the Secre-
tary of the G.P.O. and a British importation like the
Editor of the Dust Bin. A tableau of the anti-Irish
post office representing Horatius of Riverstown in the
dust and Egerton, the Britisher, of Dublin, making a
last stand for Englishism in the Post Office service of
this country, would be a welcome feature of next Sun-
day’s procession.

Again, as Father Brennan, of Killarney, a front-rank
man in Irish Ireland, suggests, the Language Week
should mark a special assault on the anti-Irish Post
Office  Give it to them hot. Even though a few
thousand unnecessary stamps were bought from the
British Government, the money would be well expended
if it made a stir in the Post Office service; it would
help to ram the Post Office’s anti-Irish spite down its
own throat.

Now that St. Patrick’s Day is a recognised holiday,
and the action of his Grace the Archbishop in conjunc-

+ tion with the other forces working that way has probably

knocked the ground from under the feet of the licensed
spongers on St. Patrick in Dublin, it would be a good
thing if a leading note in the great language procession
-—we hope the greatest that was ever seen in the streets
of Dublin—were a note of defiant reply to the anti-
Irish action of the anti-Irish Post Office authorities.
Irishmen are specially good at fighting against a tangible
objective; Egerton, the imported Secretary of the
G.P.0., Dublin, has supplied a seasonable “Aunt Sally”
for the wits of the Language Procession, and it would
be a pity not to avail of the sport. The Language Pro-
cession in Dublin is the annual triumphal march of Irish
Ireland in the metropolis. The heart of it beats with
the determined thud of a trans-Atlantic liner’s engine,
but it has, and wisely, its lighter and humorous sides
also. The anti-Irish Post Office offers good treatment
for those who are looking after the lighter side as well
as for those whose teeth are set in the cause. Down
with the anti-Irish Post Office by all means; but it
would be also a healthy and a wholesome thing to make
Dublin laugh at it. Egerton, from Britain, might as
well attempt to drain the Atlantic with an imported
British sorter’s hat, as stop the full tide of Irish Ireland ;
.and he ought to be told so, and well told so, during
Language Week.

The following occurs in the Lenten Pastoral of his
Eminence Cardinal Logue:—“ It is to be fearcd that
newspapers, indiscreetly conducted, contribute largely to
the mischief. It is certain that, with very few excep-
tions, they encourage, propagate, and maintain the gam-
bling spirit among the people ; but for this they claim a
kind of chartered right.” ~Almost, needless to say, the
ha'penny Independent climbed the Pastoral kopje on
Monday. However, in view of the engagement of the
British lady tipster 1t had to pick its steps carefully so
that it would not bark its shins against this sharp
boulder ; so, as might be expected, there is not a word in
the Independent article concerning his Eminence’s refer-
ence to the part that newspapers play in pandering to
and stimulating the awful betting evil that afflicts this
poor land. His Grace, the Archbishop of Dublin, also
refers to the betting evil in his Pastoral in the following
words : —* The ruinous practices of betting, and of gam-
bling in its various forms, which have so rapidly gained
ground in our midst, still continue to strengthen their
hold upon vast numbers of our people, even upon many
of the comparatively poor. It is deeply to be regretted
that, in thig matter, bazaars organised for religious and

3 THE LEADER.

charitable purposes have not always been free from re-
proach. It is a great scandal that practices which in
dheir nature are demoralising, especially when they have
‘been shown by experience to exercise a demoralising in-
fluence amongst our people, should be resorted to for the
purpose of raising money for works of religion and of
charity.”

‘We hope we may expect that every drink-shop in Dub-
lin will be closed during this St. Patrick’s Day as a con-
sequence of the exhortation of his Grace, the Archbishop.
The public opinion of Irish Ireland secured in previous
years the closing of a very large proportion of the grog-
shops, but so long as a considerable number of the
:meaner bungery owners sponged on St. Patrick the
permanence of even the partial closing was not assured.
His Grace’s interference in the matter is most opportune,
and if it results—as we believe it will result—in the clos-
ing of every public-house in Dublin on next St. Patrick’s
Day, then a precedent will have been established, and we
‘may reasonably look forward to the closing of the public-
houses on St. Patrick’s Day in Dublin as a matter of
course -just as we look forward to the closing of the big
business establishments of the city. We quote the refer-
ences to this matter in his Grace’s Pastoral in full : —
“ The gratifying observance of the festival of our
National Apostle as a general holiday is attended with
one serious drawback. In certain districts, not a few of
the houses licensed for the sale of intoxicating drink are
still kept open on that day. This continues to be done
although a very considerable number of the proprietors
of licensed houses close their establishments in honour
of the festival. In doing so, they do their part towards
securing that the observance of the national holiday
shall not be marred by intemperance amongst our people,
It is lamentable that the effort thus made in so good a
cause should be frustrated, as, to a large extent, it must
‘be, by the selfish action of those members of the licensed
irade who, setting the healthy public opinion of the
city at defiance, seek to make the praiseworthy action
of others an occasion of profit to themselves. 1t would
seem that the licensed houses that are kept open on St.
Patrick’s Day are, as a rule, of the lower and less re-
putable class. But it is hard to think that the pro-
‘prietors even of such houses can continue to be deaf to
an appeal made to them in the interests both of religion
:and of nationality. A vigorous combined effort should
be made by the clergy to secure a general closing of
licensed houses on St. Patrick’s Day. It is impossible
+to suppose that there is a parish in this diocese in which
such an effort, if well organised, wouldnotbesustainedand
made effective by the active and hearty support of many
zealous helpers amongst the laity, and especially amongst
the members of our Religious Confraternities, and of an
organisation such as the Gaelic League, which now
possesses so widespread an influence amongst ourpeople.”
The remark that “It would seem that the licensed houses
that are kept open on St. Patrick’s Day are, as a rule,
of the lower and less reputable class,” must be very
stinging to those spongers on St Patrick who may
hanker after “tone.” We wonder how does Anthony
O’Grady like it?

Teip Ouna Leorgane will be a welcome feature on St.
Patrick’s Day. It will be held in the Town Hall,
““Kingstown,” and should prove a great attraction to a
great many Irish Irelanders of Dublin, as well as Kings-
4own, who will be anxious to spend the holiday in a fitbing
manner. An exhibition of Irish manufacture will be
held in connection with the Zeis; and in the evening
there will be a prize winners’ concert. Since the first
attempt was made to establish the St. Patrick’s Day holi-
day there has been a lack of suitable holiday entertain-
ments in Dublin and Neighbourhood on St Patrick’s
Day. A Feis is at once entertaining and suitable, and
the Gaelic Loague of Dunleary are to be congratulated
Zor affording this opportunity {o Irish Irelanders in and
near the metropolis of enjoying themselves in a suitable
manner on St. Patrick’s Day.

We are informed that the authorities of the Good
Shepherd Convent, Waterford, buy the soap for their

laundry work exclusively from English manufacf,me_ls.
Is Lhis>ovcrsight or what? In the worst days pr Irish
manufacture, before the LEADER §barted, ]jn.s soag
manufacturers were able to fight foreign. coml_)ebmon, z;n i
in view of the progress which the Trish soap industry ha

made since then, there can be no doubb but that 11t; czil;
defy its foreign competitors on its mfants: Why ill?l)\ d
an Irish laundry turn its back on this Irish industry!

There is no abatement in the 'fdiplomacy"'of somel:(’f
those connected with the Gaelic League in Dublin.
There was a conference in connection with the Lmnguagg
Week held at the City Hall, on Thursday last, over
which Mr. John MacNeill, one of yhe Vlce—_I’remdf:nts of
the League presided. It was evidently '-mPOSSH.’]? V;
carry on this useful and important conference mtmul
some of the speakers having a shriek ab the bold, bf‘];;
foul and nefarious organ of Irish Ireland. We see by
the Freeman report that Mr. P. T. Daly, T.C., a Tin
Pike man—we are not sure that he has not recently
somersaulted and become a King’s Most Excellent Ma-
jesty, the Lords and Commons of Treland man—said : —

They should take the occasion to show the gept]eme!l
who endeavoured to impede the success of the Irish Lan-
guage Week how little their influence was by making
the fund a greater success than ever on former occa-
sions.” We take it that this is levelled at our own
foul Judge Keoghist heads. Now we do not sce that
the chairman, Mr. John MacNeill, brought this young
man to order for his attempt to injure the Language
Fund by trying to alienate Leaderites by this wild
talk. Tt is a pity that a young man of his calibre
should be intrusted with the proposing of the first resolu-
tion, and it shows into what hands any organisation
may partly fall if the public are not vigilant, and those
whose thankless duties it is to criticise are not bold and
fearless. Now we have no doubt but that this young
man is an honest and worthy young man, and sincerely
believes that we have cloven hoofs inside our boots. We
make no reflection on his bona fides, buv it is only fair
that we should point out that he is rather an interested
party. This Mr. Daly, T.C., is working manager of
An Clo Cumann, a printing firm in Dublin.

Several members of the Executive of the Gaelic League
are shareholders of the Company of which Mr. P. T.
Daly is a paid servant; on the face of it, therefore,
he is not exactly an uninterested party. Further, the
oflicial organ of the Gaelic League, that in recent years
cost the Language Fund £1,091 7s. 0d., is printed by
this firm of which Mr. P. T. Daly, T.C. is manager, and
in which several members of the Executive are in-
terested ; therefore—though we are sure Mr. P. T.
Daly, T.C., is conscientiously convinced that we have
cloven hoofs inside our boots—on the face of it he is
somewhat of an interested party,and his wild talk will be
duly measured by our thoughtful and level-headed
readers. Why Mr. John MacNeill as chairman of the
conference allowed him to talk wildly against us is
Mr. John MacNeill’s own business.

According to the F'reeman, another speaker at the
conference attacked our foul selves. This speaker was a
Mr. A. J. Nicolls.  According to the report in the
I'reeman, Mr. Nicolls said :—“They had perverse carp-
ing and cynical criticism from a weekly periodical.
That periodical often complained that its name was not
mentioned. Well, he was not going to advertise it by
giving its name, but he supposed they all knew the name,
If that periodical could have damned the Trish Language
Collection it would have been damned already. But tie
collection was not damned.” This sort of talk was
evidently allowed without any rebuke from the chairman
of the meeting. We suppose it was “cynical ariticism”
lo comment on the fact shown by the figures in the
published accounts that the official organ cost in sub-
sidies, in recent years, a sum of £1,091! Wa take it
we ‘“carped” when we asked when we might expect the
reporh and statements! Now we turn to the Directory
and find that Mr. Nicolls who was allowed to indulge in
bhis sort of twaddle against the independent organ of

Irish Ireland, is.employed in one of the offices at Dublin

Castle! We are sure he is a very estimable man, and
gives good services to Dublin Castle for his salary;
but is there not a touch of broad unconscious humour in
the fact of the independent organ of Irish Ireland being

harangued at from a Gaelic League platform by a Dublin
Castle official.

Our readers will remember that the sprightly monthly
journal, Inisfail, had a controversy with the official
organ of the Gaelic League. We predicted in our issue
of February 11th that “if the blows of Znisfail came too
sharp the official organ may get out of the difficulty by
taking them lying down.” ™ It appears that the official
organ has run away from Znesfail : bebber for it, perhaps,
that it had never tackled our contemporary, and “ kept
saying nothing.” ZInisfail is published from the offices
of the London Gaelic League—it is printed in Ircland—
and is either the official or unofficial organ of that great
centre of Gaelic League activity in England. The March
Number of Inisfail is very interesting.  One item is
headed “ dn Claidheamh runs away ;" the paragraph is
as follows:—“ We are grievously disappointed in An
Claidheamh Soluis.  To clear the air, and to fix thought
upon the essentials of nationality we introduced ideas and
principles into our discussion on Gaelicism with our con-
temporary. But ideas frightened it, and after a hasty
exhibition of petulance it ran away last month. Now,
for the credit of the cause we cannot permit this sort of

_thing. We must make our official friend meet and
masterideas. We shall keep our eyes on its lapses, call it
to account in due course, and make it try to justify itself
in a style more worthy of an intellectual movement. It
is losing inspiration, and needs shaking up, and a liberal
infusion of ideas. But it must not get cross and run
away again. It has a great mission to live up to, and
we want to see it worthy thereof.” Now this from the
official or semi-official organ of the Gaelic League in
London is startling.  Perhaps the editor of Inisfail,
whoever he may be, is also an embryo Judge Keogh!
Mr. Morris, of the Executive, had better look after him.
Perhaps, this “perverse carping and cynical criticism”
from Inisfail is intended to damn the Irish Language
Collection, and Mr. A.. J. Nicoll, of Dublin Castle, had
better come to the rescue. According to Inmisfail, the
sprightly official or semi-official organ of the London Gae-
lic League, ““ideas frightened ”* the dear official organ in
Dublin that was too hoighty-toighty to notice us, and
“after a hasty exhibition of petulance it ran away last
month.” Inisfail, it will have been seen, also says:—
“ Now, for the credit of the cause, we cannot permit this
sort of thing. We must make our official friend meet
and master ideas. We shall keep our eyes on its lapses,
call it to account in due course, and make it try to jus-
‘tify itself in a style more worthy of an intellectual move-
ment.” Well, if we are Judge Keoghs Z/nisfail must be
edited by Le Carron! Mr. P. T. Daly, T.C., should pro-
ceed at once to London, accompanied by Mr. A. J.
Nicolls, of Dublin Castle The Irish Language Cause is
surely being undermined by this foul organ of the London
Gaelic League! We are only playing second fiddle in
treacherous iniquity to Inasfail.

There has been quite a controversy in the Dundalk
Democrat about the LEADER ; it arose out of the anony-
mous fulminations written by a Mr. Henry Morris, who
is aleo a member of the Executive of the Gaelic League.
‘We note that this Mr. Henry Morris, writing as “we” in

our Dundalk contemporary, lets fly to the extent of .

about two columns, in the issue of February 25th, by
way of reply to two letters that had appeared in a pre-
vious issue of that paper. It is nono of our business to
intrude very much in the controversy going on in
another paper, but there are a few things in the two
columns of notes to which we have referred that amused
us, and which may also amuse our readers. The writer
of the notes, as we have said, is a member of the Execu-
tive, and, therefore knows something of the inner
working. He evidently knows why the mighty official
organ, that cost the Language Fund £1,091 in recent
ars, turng up ite haughty nose at us; what is more,
¢ has blurted out his knowledge. We read :—Now
R BT

D AR

THE LEADER. 3

as 1o the silence of the Claidheamh Solwis.  The
Claidheamh. Soluis is silent in pursuance of a policy
which we think is wrong. The editor of the Claidheamh,
we believe, holds that because the journalistic style of
the LEADER is of a vulgar, scurrilous type, he c:xm;ot
enter into controversey with it. But, even accepting
this summing up of the situation, if the editor of the
Claidheamh were attacked by a chimney sweep, would
he walk on and allow the sweep to continue the atback,
lest ho might get soiled and blackened in the melee!
He would not, neither should he, we hold, allow the
LeapER’s attacks to go unchallenged, even though he
might be called some ugly nicknames, and have a lot of
coarse fun poked at his head.” All this is really de-
lightful. So we are too “vulgar” and “scurrilous” to:
be spoken to by the highly respectable and very “tony’
official editor! And the writer of the notes in our
Dundalk contemporary, who is shocked at our “scur—
rility,” couples us neatly, as it will have been observed,
with a chimney sweep! The best thing of all, however,
is the suggestion that the official editor is afraid to notice
us, as if he did, we might give him a nickname! Pictl}re
a man going into Irish public life weighed down with
a morbid dread that some one would give him a nick-
name! Picture the official editor being offered a brief
in the Courts. We see him in our mind’s eye weighing:
up the probabilities of being called a nickname by the
opposing counsel ; if Tim Healy were on the other side,
he might consider the risk too great, and might decline
the brief. Now if the official editor is really in dread of
a nickname, he has no reason to be thankful to this
Mr. Morris for letting the cat out of the bag; for it is
almost like offering a prize to the country for the best
nickname for the official editor.

Here is a grave indictment from this Mr. Morris:—
“Are not the editorial columns of the LEADER master-
pieces of vituperation? Is there a single Irish paper
has invented and used more nicknames during the
four and a half years of its existence than the LEADER
has done? Why it would fill columns of this paper if we
made a compilation of all the nicknames the LEeaDER
has invented and made use of.” Well, we certainly will
make a clean breast of it, and admit that we did invent
some nicknames in our time. We believe it was we
who nicknamed a certain portion of this nation “Irish
Ireland.” We certainly have it on our conscience that
we invented “Sourface” and “Saved” ; we first nicknamed
the publicans of this country “Bung,” and called a type
of manufacturers “Dark Brothers.” We nicknamed a
certain class of writing—perhaps this Mr. Morris is
getbing fidgety here—we nicknamed a certain class of
writing “raimeis”—oh, no doubt about it, we have been
great sinners in the nicknaming line and no mistake.
And the worst of it is that the nicknames we have in-
vented so far are, we trust, nothing to those that we
hope we will be inspired to invent as time goes on.

A fair indication of the calibre and brains and level-
headedness of this correspondent of our Dundalk con-
temporary who is a member of the Executive of the
Gaelic League, may be judged by one particularly bright:
suggestion. He is talking of “‘some friends,” whoever
they may be, of the LEapER, and he says:—“Since
then these have avowed the intention, and apparently
still cherish the idea, of destroying the Claidheamls
Soluis and making the LEADER the mouthpiece of the
Gaelic League.  Ience those attacks on the official
organ, ostensibly in the interests of the public.” Of
course this twaddle need only be printed to be proved
absurd; but beyond its inherent absurdity it shows a
disposition to see “plots” and “schemes” and such like,
a disposition, unhappily, too prevalent in this half-slave,
and over-suspicious country, and which causes a lot of
avoidable mischief. It is the first we ever heard of
this “plot” of the LEADER to destroy the official organ
and become the mouthpiece of the Gaelic League; and
it appears to us that as the sole persons responsible for
the paper, that if there was a “plot” knocking around,
that we ought to have heard of it! Oh, these “plots.”
Now we believe it is generally admitted, and this Me.

36 THE LEADER.

Moris who is a schoolmaster, will probably agree, that
the whole is gréater than the part. We are the organ
of Irish Treland, with a great circulation all over the
country; the official jounnal is the official organ of a
part of Irish Ireland, with, necessarily, a restricted
circulation ; a “plot” on behalf of the organ of the whole
to become the organ of a part, might be a fit subject
for opera-bouffe; but it is a wonder this national
schoolmaster, who is on the Executive of the League,
would not have enough brains to avoid catching hold of
such & “plot” as that. Let him try again. Did he ever
hear of the great plot hatched in the Z'reeman office for
procwring the dismissal of the editor of the Skibbereen
Eagle, and the filling of that great post of national im-
portance by Brayden and Sexton? But perhaps he is
yet only a ‘prentice hand at the great Irish industry of
unearthing “plots.”

Mr. Morris of the Executive, who has gained some
accidental and passing notoriety by shouting “ Judge
Keogh ” and other things at us, has, as we have said,
drawn a controversy round his ears in the pages of our
contemporary the Dundalk Democrat. There are two
letters in our contemporary of March 4th. The LEADER
has not braved the enemies of Ireland for four years and
a half that it could be wildly attacked in the pages of
such a representative provincial paper as the Dundalk
Democrat without provoking some vigorous retorts. In
one of the letters after a reference to some of the nick-
names we invented, we read :—“This, then, has been one
portion of the LEADER'S work—and well it did that
work.  Its editorials are indeed masterpieces—not,
though, of vituperation. The ‘Johnnies’” and sours and
bigots weuld, no doubt, term it vituperation, and it all
would be personified vulgarity to the Rathmines gentry.
But no Irish Trelander would, I think, share the opinion
of these anti-Irish nobodies. It is ever lawful to hold
the mirror up to nature. Those who can do so know
more than their fellows, wield a great power, and, if
they wield it wisely and fearlessly, are and have ever
been regarded the benefactors of their country. I say
deliberately that I have never read in the LEADER out-
bursts of vituperation or indulgence in personalities.
No, not even when it was itself attacked on a few occa-
sions, and attacked in such a style as would bring dis-
credit and scorn on even unreflecting boys. It pilloried
types and classes, and occasionally individuals. But
not upon themselves but on their views, theories, or
practices it set and fixed the label whereby they ever
shall be known.”

In the course of the second letter in the Dundalk
Demaocrat we read the following, the mild irony of parts
of which we specially admire:—“In this controversy
one fact, stands out clearly, one fact that appears to me
to be the cause of all the noise—the Claidheamh Solwis
boycotts the LeapEr. Why? Your contributor’s re-
ference to vulgarity and sweeps is not a sufficient ex-
planation. Let me show this. About a year ago, I
remember the cditor of the Claidheamh allowed the word
LEADER to appear in his columns and also condescended
to answer some of its criticisms. Now wasn't the
LEADER as scurrilous and as vulgar then as now, and I
expect, the C'laidheamh was every bit as refined. Then,
recently. the Claidheamh had occasion to refer to an
article that appeared in the LEADER, and it referred to

that article as appearing in a ‘contemporary.”  There:

was no occasion for controversy here, no reason to
avoid the scurrilous tongue of the LEADER; bub I sup-

so the name of the low-down sheet would defile the
immaculate columns of the Claidheamh] Such childish-
ness! Again, the LEADER, more than a month ago
asked a few simple, straight questions; asked them in
a fair, open way, without a;r'clty seurrility or any display
of vulgarity. I think the Claidheamh had a right to
answer them, and if it had I suppose we would
have no. such. row as this. But then, who would mind
the questions of a sweep? A sweep is all right when he
cleans your chimneys and ventilates your house, bub
to address you in public—ugh! The LEADER has ven-

.tilated the Irish Ireland house fairly well, and I sup-

MarcH 11, 1905.

pose as long as it keeps on the old clothes it worked so
well in, it must be content with a sweep’s treatment.
Now, as an humble Gaelic Leaguer, I ask—Why does
the Claidheaml Soluis boycott the LeApER and at the
same time dub another weekly organ its 'cpnftere? As
I have shown, the refinement of the editor is no answer.
In my humble judgment the Claidheamh and at;s, con-
frere’ are under the influence of the same clique.

We make no apology for quoting two iurbh‘?r para-
graphs from this letter in reply to some of the P?}"t‘s

Might we add that if the Clarvear Soluip were an
ordinary paper run at one man’s risk or several men’s
risk, that we would have no more complaint at its ig-
noring our questions, the while it called a rabid poli-
tical organ its “ confrere,” that we would at the
silly boycott of ourselves by the Dublin “truly na-
tionals.”  The Dublin “truly nationals” have made
themselves ridiculous by their boycott of us, but they
are quite within their right in boycotting us. They do
so at their own profit or their own loss. But the CLarveam
Sotuip is not a private venture ;it is an official organ of a
League that, to say the least—and our readers know it
in their hearts—we have done more to advance than any
other single agency in the country ; it is the official organ
of a non-sectarian and non-political League, and has, dur-
ing a period covered by the accounts of two years and
ten months, received a total subsidy of £1,091 out of the
funds; we say that the official organ has gone outside its
sphere, and has compromised the organisation of which
it purports to be the official mouthpiece by attempting
to ostentatiously ignore us at the same time that it was
referring to an ex-Tin Pike organ as its “ confrere.” This
inevitably leads to the conclusion to which one of the
correspondents in the controversy in the Dundalk Demo-
crat arrived—“ In my humble judgment the Claidheamh
algd its ‘confrere’ are under the influence of the same
clique.” In the name of Irish Ireland we call upon the
President and Vice-Presidents and Executive of the Gae
lic Ifeague to look to this matter, and guard against the
official organ seriously compromising the Gaelic League
Organisation. At the same time we may point out that
the dangerous bog into which the official organ has been
tun by its editor does. not constitute, by any means, the
whole mafter of our recent criticism. g

Our advertisers are familiar with the name of Kevin
J. Kenny. Mr. Kenny has been connected with the ad-
vertising department of the LEADER almost since the
start of the LEADER. He was a precocious boy, for at the
age of thirteen, we understand, he ran an”advertising
agency. The display of such precocity suggests a go-
ahead, enterprising spirit, and now that Mr. Kenny has
had about four years and a half connection with the
LEADER it is not surprising that one so enterprising

- should wish to fight: out all for himself. Mr. Kenny is the
sole proprietor of that valuable handbook, “The Irish
Manufacturers’ Directory and Year Book,” the 1905
issue of which has just been published. He has taken
offices ati No. 2 Lower Abbey Street, where he intends
carrying on the business of publisher and advertising
agent. He was a very successful business man during
his long connection with the LEADER, and now that he
has started for himself we are sure his agreeable address
and keen business qualities will win success for him. We
sincerely wish him every prosperity.  Our new adver-
tisement manager is Mr. W. Henebery, with whom our
advertisers will, we are sure, continue to find a pleasure
in doing business.

A correspondent has sent us a copy of the Orange
Belfast News Letter of March 4th. It contains about a
page report of the proceedings of something or other en-
titled “ Ulster Unionist Council.” We have something
better to do with our time besides reading through all
that stuff; but our correspondent !kindly underlined
some parts of it. Amongst those present was a Denis
Henry, K.C. The Chairman of this meeting, one Colonel
M‘Calmont, M.P., in the course of his remarks, said :—
“His Excellency, the Lord Lieutenant of Ireland had
‘made the remark that Ireland should be governed ac-
cording to Irish ideas. They dissented from that alto-
ether (loud applause).” There is the “Irish” Orange
amb for you. What about all creeds and classes now !
Perhaps since a Gaelic League meeting advertised in
Ardilaun’s Orange Ezpress in Dublin another “tolerance-
proving” Gaelic Leaguer ought to advertise in the Bel-
fast News Letter. A Mr. E., M. Archdale said:—“1It
needed no words of his to assure his friends, the Ulster
Unionist members, that the whole of Ulster was united
behind them, and not only Ulster, but the entire
Unionists of Ireland, irrespective of creed.” That was
a rather nasty slap at the * tame ” Unionist Cawtholics.

Our article “Irish Policies for the Irish People,” and
subsequent articles and references have evidently upset
the Green Hungarian Band. We hear that the indus-
trial Don Quixote, Alderman Cole, lectured on the
King’s Most Excellent Majesty, the Lords and Com-
mons of Treland Policy, or, to be explicit, on the Re-
nunciation Act, 1783, recently and received a severe
heckling. 'We notice now that the ex-Tin Pike organ
is dropping the term Hungarian policy; our logical and
humorous fire was too much for that latest imitation
and importation, for we see a reference in last week’s
issue to “the practical application of the Sinn Fein
policy—the policy so successtully used by the Hungarians
in their contest with Austria—to Ireland.” So it is the
Sinn Fein  policy now; that is the old Irish Ireland
policy of the LEaDER. We commend our contemporary’s
change of front. In August, 1901, we had a stand at
the Horse Show, and put up Sinn Fein there. In the
course of our notes on the matter in our issue of 31st
August, 1901, we wrote, “There will not be much Irish
there, though our signboard will have Sinn Fein upcn
it and, no doubt, many a genteel missy from the vil-
lages will giggle before it and say : ‘How awfully funny ;’
and many a nation-killer will feel a qualm of conscience.
We don'’t like paying the un-Irish Royal Dublin Society
the cost of our modest stand, but they bar the way
with their tariffs, and it serves our turn to go in there.
Therefore we go in—mildly laughing at it before we
enter.” The Sinn Fem policy is our own policy, and
in working in Ireland from what is to what ought to be,
it is more than probable that many things will be done
in Ireland somewhat similar to things that have already
happened in other countries that emerged from thrall-
dom into full or partial independence. “Irish Policies
for the Irish People,” is the only statesmanlike attitude
for Ireland. As we said in the opening article in our
first number four and a half years ago, “To us it (Ireland
a nation) means a self-governing land, living, moving,
and having its being in its own language, self-reliant,
intellectually as well as politically independent, initia-
ting its own reforms, developing its own manners and
customs, creating its own literature out of its own dis-
tinctive consciousness, working to their fullest capacity
the material resources of the country, inventing, criti-
cising, attemptng and doing.” Since our first number
we have always stuck to the principle of Irish policies
for the Irish people. We would be in favour of drawing
off the Parliamentary Party from “the floor” if that
matter were desirable and within the range of practical
politics. During Parnell’s time the policy of attending
the floor was, in conjunction with agitation at home, ab
least, effective; since then the Britishers have over-
hauled the Parliamentary machine, and the tactics of
Parnell and Biggar are no longer possible.

Whenever it may be thought desirable to draw off
the Party it will first be necessary to convince the con-

stituents who elect the party of that desirability; and
that cannot be done in the twinkling of an eye. The
development of national sibuations does not usually pro-
«wceed by sudden revolution, but slowly at best. A great,
deal of progress has been made in recent years, and the
quicker the rate of progress the better we like it ; bub
at every stage of our development we must stick to the
principle of Irish policies for the Irish people. It is
bad enough to have shoals of Scotch “‘experts” coming
over here to show us how to do this, that, and the other
without importing Hungarian “experts” to show us how
to be Deaks and Kossuths and so forth, and give us
oxpert advice at: the various stages of imitating a foreign
policy so as to preserve the almost perfect parallel.

We have always said, use all the power and Home
Rule that you have at any given time and work on for
more.  We have, therefore advocated Catholics and
Nationalists fighting for—not sneaking after—all the
positions of power in the country that they can get.
We have advocated combinations of Catholics as citizens
for' the defence and assertion of their civil and economic
rights against the exclusiveness of the ascendancy and
the grab-alls, for that is in a special manner, a much
needed Irish policy for the Irish people as at present,
situated. We are, as we so often say, an abnormal
country, and we must go as quickly as possible hand
over hand to normality, and from that to as much
national fulness of life and political freedom as we can
attain,

In the concluding article of M. OR's. series on
* Horace Plunkett’s Book ” last week, reference was made
to a shirt factory started by Father Cregan in Limerick.
It so happens that the day we arranged for Press the
Dublin representative of this Limerick factory called
upon us, and asked us to inspect a collection of samples
of shirts made at St. Ita’s Factory, Limerick. The
shirts are made to suit all pockets, and range from
superior shirts made of Irish flannel down to shirts that
would probably be sold retail at about 2s. 6d. or 2s. 11d.
<eaeh. As far as we could judge, they all appeared to be
excellently turned out, and each shirt is marked with the
name of the factory. We welcome this rising Limerick
industry.  Ireland could stand very many additional
shirg factories before every Irish back was covered with
an Irish shirt; and besides the home market there are
foreign markets in which Irish-made shirts have plenty
of room to find purchasers.

4 Capa,—The Post Office has declared war on the
Gaelic League. Friends of the movement should \\'(’-I~
come the combat. Prompt and vigorous action on the
part of the Gaelic League Organisation would soon
bring the Postal authorities to their knees. It would
be fatal for the Gaelic League to decline the combat.
It would be worse than foolish for them to neglect an
opportunity of demonstrating their strength against a
hostile Government Department.

This incident should be carefully considered at the
next meeting of the Coipoe Incta and some pracll_cul
course of action decided on. The great demonstration
in Dublin in connection with Language Week would give
the League an opportunity of declaring its policy. All
Gaelic League Branches and all public bodies should
be got to declare immediately, and in uncompromising
terms, against the latest piece of British impertlnen‘cu.
A formal protest should be made against the use of Irish
at the foot of recruiting posters, and the youth of the
country should be warned against this hypocritical use
of the native language on the part of an avowedly hos-
tile Government. An anti-enlisting campaign con-
ducted by the Gaelic League, would be a practical means
of demonstrating its power to the authorities of Britain.

Combined action should be taken by all friends of
the movement to show that Irish is a language in current
use.  Here in Killarney we hope to have the local
authorities dealing with some hundreds of documents
addressed wholly in Irish within the next few days.
I commend this action to other districts.

The time for parley has gone by. Irish Ireland has
already stated its demands. The Post Office has re-
plied with a challenge. Are we ready for the combat?

NATIONAL HOLIDAY IN LIMERICK. |

IMERICK Gaelic Leaguers, with the aid of the

_  other Nationalist bodies in the city, gave Ireland

a lead three years ago in bringing about the observance

of St. Patrick’s Day as a National Holiday, and as ways

and means of inducing the trade to join in the observ-

ance are being discussed in Dublin and throughout the

country just now; it will, I am sure, interest your readers

to know what the pioneers of the movement have dane,
and are doing, here this year. 3

In 1902 the movement was resisted by licensed and
non-licensed traders alike—in fact, our most influential
and determined opponents then were the monster dra-
pery houses. In 1903 and 1904 little difficulty was ex-
perienced except with the members of the trade, although
in all cases a little stir and agitation was necessary.
Without this agitation I fear that a number of traders
would find it convenient, to forget the history and estab-
lishment of the holiday. About the middle of February
each year the Gaelic League invites the co-operation of
the Temperance and other National Societies. Ward de-
putations are appointed for the purpose of canvassing
traders. The parish priests in the Diocese of Limerick,
and of other dioceses adjoining, are advised of the steps
being taken, and requested to bring the holiday under
the notice of their parishioners, so that their business
would be done in Limerick on the days preceding the
holiday, and that no disappointment or inconvenience
may arise. The local newspapers advertise the move-
ment, and observe the holiday thémselves by stopping
their issues for the day. Shopkeepers display the win-
dow bills supplied by the Holiday Committee for weeks
before, and handbills are distributed on the two market
days preceding the holiday advising the country people
to observe it, and remain away from town on that day.

Another step,’ the importance of which, I fear, is not
appreciated in other places is that the railvay company
have been asked, and invariably consent, not to run ex-
cursions into town on St. Patrick’s Day. The reason is
obvious. - The presence of a number of visitors in a town
is a direct incentive to traders to open, and, in fact, if
not stopped would be the means of forcing many traders
to open, who would otherwise willingly enough keep their
premises closed.

All this organisation, and the working of it in detail,
mean a good deal of work and some expense, but in the
present state of public opinion in Ireland we find it ne-
cessary year after year. Many men—Catholics and Na-
tionalists—who will shut up their shopsandsuspend busi-
ness with unfailing regularity every time an English Bank
I-.Iollda.y comes round, need the force of a popular agita-
tion to remind them of St. Patrick’s Day. Others who
say that they are willing to lay down their lives in de-
fence of their faith and country find it difficulty to ab-
stain on one'day from a pint of porter for their sake!

THE IRISH POST OFFICE.

]T is not more than three years ago since the Irish
Post Office Clerks, happily, decided to cut the
cable that bound them to Cross Channel Service Associa-
& tions. Previous to that time the Postmaster General
5 “and the “ Saved” staffs of the Secretary’s and Surveyor’s
Offices could do what they liked with the Post Office in
Ireland. Postmasters, Engineers, Superintendents, etc.,
arrived into this country as regular and as numerous as
“the mails,” and nobody seemed to care much how or
Rraee why it all happened. To say the least, the poor sub-
servient Irish had no strength or independence of spirit.
They treated themselves more or less as an inferior class
and seemed to be very well satisfied that they existed
at all. In this respect they differed in no way from the
generality of Trishmen. It isonly a case in point which
clearly indicates those slavish and inert characteristics
which had grown into us.

With the establishment of an Irish Association, the
Irish at last began to wake up and to question these im-
portations, and many other things which English Asso-
ciations with “dead and alive” Irish branches never
troubled about. It is a good thing this first sign of re-
turning consciousness, and as the Irish have now fairly
started on the road towards agitation on their own ac-
count, the following shorti review of the situation will
perhaps be in seasonable time:—

Regarding the agitation for increase of pay and con-
ditions of work, I shall say nothing, as these questions
_ chiefly concern the Postmaster-General and the Staff.
- I will, however, remark that the deferred pay system”

is the most hollow mockery ever devised. It is briefly

this:—That out of an officer’s pay there is stopped,

weekly, a certain percentage which, on rebi.remenb, forms

pension allowance. If the person dies in the service,

accumulated deferred pay is not given to his wife

of a good Government, there are, I expect, many mathe-

entirely unsuspicious of. .

About the promotions.—This is something that will
interest the public, and will also be beneficial to those
who are agitating by way of opening their eyes to some-
thing which they may perhaps be not aware of. In the
past the Irish were treated as nothing. At the present
the Postmaster-General has become in a small way
reasonable. There are many conditions which militate
against Irishmen, and I will endeavour to enumerate a
few and to explain them:—

(1). The spirit of serfdom and indifference which dis-
tinguished the older generation does still exercise a
depressing influence over the younger men. There is
a tendency, with them, to fall into the old groove, and
example and surroundings help to keep them in it. The
result is that they also become careless and indifferent
as to the future, and neglect to take a wholesome in-
terest in the business, and when a man loses interest in
himself there is no hope for him.

(2). The great majority of the superior positions being
in the hands of the anti-Irish, there is little encourage-
ment to Irishmen (especially Catholics) to work for
anything.  The sceptre of authority is wielded by
those people as if they appeared to be convinced that
the Irish are really an inferior breed, and, therefore,
not supposed to look above their present level. Then,
too, influence gets everything, and all the influence is
with the anti-Irish. To give the Postmaster-General
his due, there is a motice in staring black type inserted
in each issue of the Post Office Circular to the effect
that “Influence does no good and should not: be sought.”
Of course it is read by everybody as an excellent bit of
sly humour, at least, nobody takes it seriously, and the
good old game goes on, and eventually works out to the
good of “Saved garrison,” who chuckle at the joke in the
Official Circular.

Only those Irish who happen to have patronage (they
are generally shoneens) are gifted with anything like
easy jobs in which they manage to come off with clear
records. These, in order to keep their mean skins safe,
become just as antagonistic to the Irish as the “Saved,”
so that the mere Irish have nothing to do bub tread the
ordinary thorny paths exposed to the lashes of the
people above.

The effects of these conditions are that there is no
incentive to progress or enterprise and a state of inertia
follows. What the younger men have to do is (1) to
shake off the inertia and indifference, and to make up
their minds to take an active interest in getting a grip
of overy branch of their business. If they are denied
opportunities to do so they must fight for them. (2)
‘When they have done that they will then, and not till
then, be in fit form to attack the strongholds of the

didn’t, occur to himself that he was telling the truth.
And I daresay ’tis the same with the average English
‘writer about Ireland. Strange to say though, we our-
selves don’t seem to see this. Being, in the main, more
or less innocent, and more or less honest, we have, from
‘being so persistently reminded of our faults, with des-

) . unexpected burs of loyalty, and so some time ago, having

perate candour come to think ourselves as black as we
are painted. Which isn’t what a politician would call
“business.” If the Irish cause were finally successful,
wo should hear only of the Irish virtue that conduced to
and deserved that success. Not being successful, we
are taunted with being slaves of the worst vices to which
defeat may be attribubed. As far as the taunt of fac-
tiousness is concerned, I propose to argue that we are
not more factious than the neighbours. As the Gar-
rison are enjoying a little falling-out on their own ac-
count just ab present, the raising of the question may
not be altogether irrelevant. z

"Tisn't the fashion to argue an Irish question without
appealing to history. Well, as dead against us as his-
tory appears to be in this matter, I don’t know that 'tis
any more than a case of “an c-e acd piop blailceap
cop aip.”’ Let us have a look at an episode or two in
history. First, as to the way history has of looking at
things Irish:—

At the Revolution, Nationalist Ireland was united
for James. Well, but such unity was labelled “insular
sectarianism!” Take another case. When Flood and
Grattan quarrelled all the misfortune that followed
was laid to the charge of Irish disunion. That's all
right perhaps, but just about the same time, two
thousand miles or so further west from England, amidst
dissensions which tried the patience of the “Second Cin-
cinnatus,” “a giant nation sprang from the waters of
the Atlantic,” etc.!—nursed to life by the accident of
its isolation. Or again, look at this:—In ‘98 our -
veterate turbulence was taught a lesson in symmetrical
conduct by enlightened Beresfords with triangles, but
a few years later the same turbulence, galvanized into
one heaving wave by the magic of the Liberator’s wand,
is called subservience and other nice names! And so
on, down to the other day, when the Parnell split con-
victed us finally and irrevocably as incorrigible, so much
so, indeed, that the concord-loving Liberals weie
shocked at our conduct and haven’t been the better
of the shock ever since!

Now let us examine one or two historic facts for ow-
selves. We may pass over the old Celtic times—when
few of our princes died other than what the wits call
a “ natural” death—meaning a death not in their beds,
with the remark that other countries were as torn as was
Treland with civil strife in those far-off days. Coming
down a few centuries, we have Spencer’s opinion of us.
Well, Spencer had not many soft words for us, but his
worst description, so often quoted; that the Irish were
“cruel, bloody, licentious, swearers full of deadly execu-
tion,” refers—what the quoters generally forget to
mention—only to soldiers billeted in time of war. (I
fancy our present-day Tommies wouldn’t consider them-
selves complimented if they were let off as lightly as
that—aye, in times of peace even). Sir John Davies’
testimony is more to the point. “There is no nation
of people under the sun,” says Sir John, “ that doth
love equal and indifferent justice more than the Irish
or will rest better satisfied with the execution thereof,
although it be against themselves, so they may have the
benefit and protection of the law.” We might well, per-
haps, bear this latter testimony in mind while reading
the boisterous rocord of Irish factionism in the time of
the “Two Hughs,” and of Eoghan Ruadh and so on.
That, together with remembering that England had her
Woars of the Roses, her Great Rebellion, her Revolution,
and such like. And in later times, especially during
the just past century, the wonder is, when you come to
think of it, that O’Connell and Parnell managed to
hold the people logether as well as they did and so
long. Only that the stuff for holding together was in
the people, they could not have been organized so
readily.

No, this idea that we are naturally factious is not the
only idea that we have been bluffed into sharing. I

HE exposures of the LeADER regarding the bigoted

and Freemason influences at work in the Irish
railway and bank services to the detriment of Catholic
employees, are probably the most valuable journalistic
work performed for the good of Ireland in recent times.
There are, however, other branches of the public ser-
vice where the boycotting of Catholics is carried on even
more relentlessly and systematically than in the great
monopolist: companies with which the Leaper has so
effectively and successfully dealt. Take, for instance,
the Prison’s Service and we have in Ireland, under the
Prisons’ Board management, eighteen gaols, including
inebriate and convict establishments, and in these the
positions of greatest emolument are, practically speak-
ing, Protestant preserves. The four principal estab-
lishments, graded as first class, are Maryborough,
Mountjoy, Belfast, and Cork, where the governors re-
ceive salaries of from £400 to £600 a year. There is
not one Catholic governor in these first-class prisons.
In connection with them are deputy governorships,
worth, on the average, about £200 a year. There is
only one Catholic deputy-governor (Belfast) who is paid
-the minimum salary of £120. Next in order are ten
second-class prisons, the governors of which draw salaries
of from £300 to £350 a year (emoluments included).
Out of these ten, only three are Catholics. Then there

Arrangements are now complete for the great services
in the Cathedral, on March 19th, at 5 p.m., and the
Archbishop has promised to assist in state. Most Rev.
Dr. O’Donnell will preach the Irish sermon, and Blshop
Tenton will officiate at Benediction. Everything points to
a magnificent success in point of numbers, and the Com-
mitlee have made every effort to draw attention to it.
All the Irish organisations are heart and soul with it,
and we anticipate the overflowing of the Cathedral. The
book of the ceremony in Irish, or Latin, and English will
be on sale at the doors, price sixpence, or may be had
post free for sevenpence after Tuesday next from the
Hon. Sec. at 9 Duke Street, Strand. Financial assis-
tance is still badly needed, which fact scarcely nceds
comment.  Unless tthe lcollection approaches three
